output.final_deliverable:
## 10 links collected from Wikipedia’s Main Page

Source page visited: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page (page heading: “Main Page”) — it contains the site navigation links such as “Contents”, “Current events”, “Random article”, etc. For example, the Main Page text includes the site header/intro “Welcome to Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia that anyone can edit.” (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page: “Welcome to\nWikipedia\n,\nthe\nfree\nencyclopedia\nthat\nanyone can edit\n.”)

Below are 10 links that are present on the Main Page, with their URL, link text, and a brief description.

> Note: The provided RAW VISIT CONTENT includes full text only for the Main Page. The additional page descriptions below rely on the retrieved visit memories’ evidence that these targets are Wikipedia navigation/help pages linked from the Main Page (the memories list them under “Links found on page …” with their anchor text). However, the dataset does **not** include the full page text for those targets, so I cannot quote those target pages verbatim.

### 1) Contents
- **Link text (anchor):** Contents
- **URL:** https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Contents
- **Evidence it’s on the Main Page:** The visited Main Page extraction lists it as an on-page link (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page, extracted links include “https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Contents”). Also confirmed in retrieved memory (Links from the Main Page include “https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Contents (Contents)”).
- **Description:** Wikipedia’s main contents hub that helps readers browse the encyclopedia by topic and provides curated navigation to subject areas.

### 2) Current events
- **Link text (anchor):** Current events
- **URL:** https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Current_events
- **Evidence it’s on the Main Page:** Listed in Main Page extracted links (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page, extracted links include “https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Current_events”). Also shown in retrieved memory (Links from the Main Page include “https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Current_events (Current events)”).
- **Description:** A portal summarizing notable ongoing and recent news events with links to related Wikipedia articles.

### 3) Random article
- **Link text (anchor):** Random article
- **URL:** https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:Random
- **Evidence it’s on the Main Page:** Listed in Main Page extracted links (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page, extracted links include “https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:Random”). Also shown in retrieved memory (Links from the Main Page include “https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:Random (Random article)”).
- **Description:** A special page that takes you to a randomly selected Wikipedia article.

### 4) About Wikipedia
- **Link text (anchor):** About Wikipedia
- **URL:** https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:About
- **Evidence it’s on the Main Page:** Listed in Main Page extracted links (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page, extracted links include “https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:About”). Also shown in retrieved memory (Links from the Main Page include “https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:About (About Wikipedia)”).
- **Description:** An overview of what Wikipedia is, how it works, and the basic principles behind the project.

### 5) Contact Wikipedia
- **Link text (anchor):** Contact Wikipedia
- **URL:** https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Contact_us
- **Evidence it’s on the Main Page:** Listed in Main Page extracted links (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page, extracted links include “https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Contact_us”). Also shown in retrieved memory (Links from the Main Page include “https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Contact_us (Contact Wikipedia)”).
- **Description:** Guidance on how to contact Wikipedia/Wikimedia or get help, including where to report issues or ask questions.

### 6) Help
- **Link text (anchor):** Help
- **URL:** https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Contents
- **Evidence it’s on the Main Page:** Shown in retrieved memory as a link on the Main Page (Links from the Main Page include “https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Contents (Help)”).
- **Description:** The main help index for using and editing Wikipedia, linking to documentation and how-to pages.

### 7) Learn to edit
- **Link text (anchor):** Learn to edit
- **URL:** https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Introduction
- **Evidence it’s on the Main Page:** Shown in retrieved memory as a link on the Main Page (Links from the Main Page include “https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Introduction (Learn to edit)”).
- **Description:** An introductory help page for new contributors, explaining editing basics and how to get started.

### 8) Community portal
- **Link text (anchor):** Community portal
- **URL:** https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Community_portal
- **Evidence it’s on the Main Page:** Shown in retrieved memory as a link on the Main Page (Links from the Main Page include “https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Community_portal (Community portal)”).
- **Description:** A central page for Wikipedia contributors, with links to collaboration areas, tasks, and community resources.

### 9) Recent changes
- **Link text (anchor):** Recent changes
- **URL:** https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:RecentChanges
- **Evidence it’s on the Main Page:** Shown in retrieved memory as a link on the Main Page (Links from the Main Page include “https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:RecentChanges (Recent changes)”).
- **Description:** A live feed showing the latest edits across Wikipedia, useful for monitoring activity and reviewing edits.

### 10) Main page
- **Link text (anchor):** Main page
- **URL:** https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page
- **Evidence it’s on the Main Page:** Shown in retrieved memory “Links found on page …” list as “https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page (Main page)”. The RAW VISIT CONTENT confirms we are on the Main Page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page shows heading “Main Page” and begins with “Welcome to Wikipedia…”).
- **Description:** Wikipedia’s front page featuring highlights like “From today’s featured article”, “Did you know…”, “In the news”, and other navigation.

## Missing evidence (limits of provided data)
- The mandate asked for “a brief description … of what the page is about” for each link, ideally backed by each target page’s own content. In the provided dataset, **only** the Main Page’s full text is included under RAW VISIT CONTENT. The “Visit each of the 10 selected links…” step was **skipped** in the execution trail, and no RAW VISIT CONTENT is provided for the linked pages—so I cannot include verbatim quotes from each linked target page to substantiate the per-page descriptions.

If you want, I can re-run with visits to each of these 10 URLs so each description can be backed with direct quotes from those pages.
output.action_summary: Collected 10 on-page links from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page (navigation/help links such as Contents, Current events, Random article, About Wikipedia, Contact Wikipedia, Help, Learn to edit, Community portal, Recent changes, and Main page). The dataset only includes full text for the Main Page, so target-page descriptions are not backed by verbatim quotes from those target pages.

action_result.content:
Welcome to
Wikipedia
,
the
free
encyclopedia
that
anyone can edit
.
290,332
active editors
7,145,614
articles in
English
From today's featured article
John Tonkin
(1902–1995) was the
premier of Western Australia
from 3 March 1971 to 8 April 1974. Born and receiving most of his education in the
Goldfields
town of
Boulder
, Tonkin became a member of the
Labor Party
and a minister in the
Willcock
,
Wise
and
Hawke
governments. He was a member of the
Western Australian Legislative Assembly
from 1933 to 1977, making him the longest-serving member of the
Parliament of Western Australia
as of 2021. Tonkin became premier after the
1971 state election
, but with a majority of only one seat. The Tonkin government's achievements included reforms in industrial relations and employment, and the passing of the
Aboriginal Heritage Act 1972
. Labor was defeated in the
1974 state election
, and Tonkin was succeeded as premier by
Charles Court
. Tonkin was made a companion of the
Order of Australia
in 1977, and has been honoured with the naming of the
Tonkin Highway
and John Tonkin College.
(
Full article...
)
Recently featured:
Galaxy
High and Low
(1963 film)
Hume MRT station
Did you know ...
Bella Ramsey
... that
Bella Ramsey
(pictured)
is the youngest person ever to be nominated twice for a Primetime Emmy lead-actress award?
... that German settler newspapers played a significant role in agitating for the
Herero and Nama genocide
?
... that British academic
Kadiatu Kanneh-Mason
quit her job to raise seven children and encouraged them to become musicians?
... that the artworks in the Louvre's
Gallery of the Five Continents
are meant to dialogue with each other?
... that
a 1998 referendum
to repeal South Carolina's unenforceable interracial marriage ban was opposed by more than 38 percent of voters?
... that the mother of a man who was murdered by
Martín Ríos
said that the case opened a social debate in Argentina on the difference between psychosis and psychopathy?
... that the excavator of the
Palazzo delle Colonne
had to publish his findings from photographs and memory after World War II interrupted the excavation and vandals destroyed the records?
... that Confederate Navy officer
Alexander F. Warley
commanded the ironclad ram CSS
Manassas
to attack two ships on which he had previously served as a U.S. Navy sailor?
... that some of the tracks on
Karrionic Hacktician
and
Skin Stripper
are only a few seconds long?
... that
Comic Beam
has published manga making fun of its low sales?
In the news
Ali Khamenei
Israel and the United States
launch strikes
on Iran,
killing
its supreme leader,
Ali Khamenei
(pictured)
, along with
other senior officials
.
A
Bolivian Air Force
Lockheed C-130 Hercules
crashes into a road
in
El Alto
, killing more than 20 people.
A military conflict
between Afghanistan and Pakistan escalates as the countries exchange cross-border strikes.
Floods
in the
Zona da Mata
region of
Minas Gerais
, Brazil, leave at least 70 people dead and thousands of others displaced.
Ongoing
:
Russo-Ukrainian war
timeline
Sudanese civil war
timeline
Recent deaths
:
Roman Kofman
Oliver Grant
MaryAnn Bin-Sallik
Sondra Lee
Ann Godoff
Shinya
On this day
March 3
:
Purim
(Judaism, 2026);
Lantern Festival
in China (2026);
Hinamatsuri
in Japan
Metallica in 2024
1776
–
American Revolutionary War
:
Samuel Nicholas
and the
Continental Marines
successfully landed on
New Providence
in the Bahamas and began a
raid of Nassau
, capturing the port the next day.
1875
– The
first indoor game
of
ice hockey
was played at the
Victoria Skating Rink
in
Montreal
by
James Creighton
and students of
McGill University
.
1933
– The
first Nazi concentration camp
was opened in
Nohra
to reduce overcrowding in prisons from the arrest of
German Communists
.
1945
– A Polish
Home Army
unit
massacred
at least 150 Ukrainian civilians in
Pawłokoma
, Poland.
1986
– The American heavy metal band
Metallica
(pictured)
released their third studio album,
Master of Puppets
, considered one of the greatest in the genre's history.
Matthias de l'Obel
(
d.
1616)
Michele Singer Reiner
(
b.
1955)
Rebecca Lancefield
(
d.
1981)
Maija Isola
(
d.
2001)
More anniversaries:
March 2
March 3
March 4
Today's featured picture
Apollo 9
(March 3–13, 1969) was the third
crewed mission
in the United States'
Apollo program
. Launched by a
Saturn V
and flown in
low Earth orbit
, the mission
flight-qualified
the
Lunar Module
, showing that its crew could fly it independently, then
rendezvous
and
dock
, as would be required for
Apollo 11
, the first crewed lunar landing. Commander
James McDivitt
, Command Module Pilot
David Scott
, and Lunar Module Pilot
Rusty Schweickart
tested systems and procedures critical to landing on the Moon. A
spacewalk
tested the extravehicular
life support backpack
. McDivitt and Schweickart, entering the Lunar Module through the docking tunnel, became the first humans to pass between spacecraft without going outside them, two months after Soviet
cosmonauts
spacewalked to transfer between
Soyuz 4
and
Soyuz 5
. Apollo 9, a complete success, was followed by
Apollo 10
, the dress rehearsal for Apollo 11. This photograph shows Schweickart operating a
Hasselblad
camera on the porch of the Lunar Module during an
extravehicular activity
on the fourth day of the Apollo 9 mission. The image was taken by Scott while standing in the hatch of the
Command Module
Gumdrop
.
Photograph credit:
David Scott
/ NASA

Kadiatu Kanneh-Mason
quit her job to raise seven children and encouraged them to become musicians?
... that the artworks in the Louvre's
Gallery of the Five Continents
are meant to dialogue with each other?
... that
a 1998 referendum
to repeal South Carolina's unenforceable interracial marriage ban was opposed by more than 38 percent of voters?
... that the mother of a man who was murdered by
Martín Ríos
said that the case opened a social debate in Argentina on the difference between psychosis and psychopathy?
... that the excavator of the
Palazzo delle Colonne
had to publish his findings from photographs and memory after World War II interrupted the excavation and vandals destroyed the records?
... that Confederate Navy officer
Alexander F. Warley
commanded the ironclad ram CSS
Manassas
to attack two ships on which he had previously served as a U.S. Navy sailor?
... that some of the tracks on
Karrionic Hacktician
and
Skin Stripper
are only a few seconds long?
... that
Comic Beam
has published manga making fun of its low sales?

Today's featured picture
Apollo 9
(March 3–13, 1969) was the third
crewed mission
in the United States'
Apollo program
. Launched by a
Saturn V
and flown in
low Earth orbit
, the mission
flight-qualified
the
Lunar Module
, showing that its crew could fly it independently, then
rendezvous
and
dock
, as would be required for
Apollo 11
, the first crewed lunar landing. Commander
James McDivitt
, Command Module Pilot
David Scott
, and Lunar Module Pilot
Rusty Schweickart
tested systems and procedures critical to landing on the Moon. A
spacewalk
tested the extravehicular
life support backpack
. McDivitt and Schweickart, entering the Lunar Module through the docking tunnel, became the first humans to pass between spacecraft without going outside them, two months after Soviet
cosmonauts
spacewalked to transfer between
Soyuz 4
and
Soyuz 5
. Apollo 9, a complete success, was followed by
Apollo 10
, the dress rehearsal for Apollo 11. This photograph shows Schweickart operating a
Hasselblad
camera on the porch of the Lunar Module during an
extravehicular activity
on the fourth day of the Apollo 9 mission. The image was taken by Scott while standing in the hatch of the
Command Module
Gumdrop
.
Photograph credit:
David Scott
/ NASA
